title: rasch — Rasch Measurement Theory in R
text: Models. Dichotomous, partial credit, rating scale Rasch models; many-facet model (Linacre 1989); extended frame reference model (Humphry 2005) — knowledge first software implementation; Bradley–Terry–Luce model paired comparisons conditional form dichotomous Rasch model, graded preferences, judge diagnostics, judge-group DIF, within-judge dependence (order carry-) effects. Estimation. Person-free pairwise conditioning throughout; sum-zero identification; sandwich standard errors (judge-clustered judges exist); anchored estimation equating; principal-components threshold parameterisation sparse categories. Test fit. log--mean-square fit residual apportioned degrees freedom, infit outfit, item-trait chi-square automatically sized class intervals per-interval detail table, class-interval ANOVA item F, threshold ordering diagnostics, calibrated composite likelihood-ratio test PCM RSM. Independence. Residual principal components model-simulated parallel-analysis reference, Smith’s person t-test magnitude estimation (Andrich 2016), Yen’s Q3 adjusted Q3* residual correlations, response-dependence magnitude logits (Andrich & Kreiner 2010), subtests, spread tests. Invariance. DIF residual analysis variance number person factors — jointly, main effects full interactions, proper within-subject (repeated-measures) error strata — DIF magnitudes logits resolved-item method, planned contrasts, automatic iterative resolution artificial DIF (Andrich & Hagquist 2012), common-item equating tests. Everything exports. Every table CSV, every plot PNG PDF, one-file HTML report whole analysis, exact R call reproducing output shown alongside .

title: rasch — Rasch Measurement Theory in R
text: Missing data allowed everywhere: estimation pairwise person measures use person’s observed items. Empty categories collapsed constant items dropped, notes recorded fit.
code: library(rasch)  # a persons-by-items data frame; item names, an ID column, and person # factors carry through the whole analysis fit <- rasch(responses, model = "PCM",              id = "person_id", factors = c("gender", "site"))  summary(fit)          # the full test-of-fit report fit$items             # locations, SEs, fit residuals, infit/outfit, chi-square fit$person            # WLE person measures with SEs and person fit score_table(fit)      # raw score to measure conversion  plot_icc(fit, "Q05", group = "gender")   # the graphical DIF display plot_pimap(fit)                          # person-item threshold distribution  dif_anova(fit)              # DIF over all factors jointly (main effects;                             # effects = "factorial" adds interactions) dimensionality_test(fit)    # Smith's residual-component t-test residual_correlations(fit)  # Yen's Q3 / Q3* local dependence  save_outputs(fit, "results/")   # every table and plot, in one call

code: rasch::run_app() # ------------------------------------------------------------- test of fit -- chisq_detail(fit, "Q05")     # per-class-interval chi-square detail ctt_table(fit)               # classical companions: facility, item-total r,                              # discrimination index, alpha, classical SEM lr_test(fit)                 # PCM vs RSM: raw + Kent-calibrated composite LR compare_fits(PCM = fit, RSM = rasch(responses, model = "RSM")) guttman_table(fit)           # scalogram with coefficient of reproducibility  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- persons -- score_table(fit, method = "mle", extremes = "extrapolated") person_extrapolated(fit)     # geometric extreme-score extrapolation                              # (Andrich & Marais 2019, ch. 10)  # ----------------------------------------------------------- independence -- dimensionality_test(fit, component = 2)   # any residual component's split dimensionality_magnitude(fit, list(setA, setB))                              # Andrich (2016): c, rho = 1/(1+c^2), and A plot_pca_biplot(fit)         # PC1 x PC2 loadings biplot plot_scree(fit)              # eigenvalues with a model-simulated reference plot_resid_cor(fit)          # Q3* heatmap (stat = "q3" for raw Q3) dependence_magnitude(fit, dependent = "Q05", independent = "Q04")                              # Andrich & Kreiner d in logits, with SE fit2 <- combine_items(fit, list(c("Q04", "Q05")))   # subtest remedy spread_test(fit2)            # spread against the binomial least upper bound  # ------------------------------------------------------------- invariance -- dif_anova(fit, sizes = TRUE) # all factors jointly; within-subject factors                              # get proper repeated-measures error strata dif_contrasts(fit)           # planned one-df questions with familywise control dif_size(fit, "Q05", by = "gender")   # DIF magnitude in logits fit3 <- split_items(fit, "Q05", by = "gender")      # resolve one item resolve_dif(fit)             # split iteratively, largest effect first,                              # until no significant DIF remains tailored_analysis(fit, chance = 0.25) # four-step guessing procedure  # ---------------------------------------------------------------- equating -- fit_eq <- rasch(responses, anchors = data.frame(item = c("Q01", "Q10"),                                                 k = c(1, NA), tau = c(-1.2, 0.9))) eq <- equate_tests(fit, fit_eq)  # drift tests through the common items plot_equate(fit, fit_eq)  # -------------------------------------------------------------- many-facet -- mf <- rasch_mfrm(ratings, person = "person", item = "criterion",                  score = "score", facets = "rater") mf$facet_effects$rater       # severities with SEs and pooled fit plot_facets(mf) mfi <- rasch_mfrm(ratings, person = "person", item = "criterion",                   score = "score", facets = "rater", interaction = "rater") mfi$interaction_effects      # item-by-rater interactions  # --------------------------------------------------------- multiple choice -- mc <- rasch(responses_raw, key = c(Q1 = "A", Q2 = "C", Q3 = "B")) distractor_analysis(mc)      # per option: n, proportion, location, pt-biserial prop <- distractor_rescore(mc)          # propose polytomous option scores mc2 <- rasch(responses_raw, key = prop$option_scores)  # ------------------------------------------------- extended frame of reference -- ef <- rasch_efrm(responses, item_sets = list(numeracy = num_items,                                              literacy = lit_items),                  groups = "year_group") ef$phi_table; ef$alpha_table # group and item-set units with SEs plot_frames(ef); plot_icc_frames(ef, "Q07")  # ------------------------------------------------------- paired comparisons -- bt <- btl(comparisons, object_a = "left", object_b = "right",           winner = "preferred", judge = "judge", order = "sequence") bt$objects; bt$judges        # locations + fit; erratic judges flag bt$dependence                # within-judge exposure and carry-over, in logits plot_btl_dependence(bt, "carry_over")   # interrogate a dependence effect btl_transitivity(bt)         # preference loops: is one scale enough? btl_dimensionality(bt)       # residual "swirl" (bimensions) = a 2nd attribute? plot_btl_scree(btl_dimensionality(bt))  # bimension scree vs a noise reference btl_dif(bt, list(panel = panel_map, experience = exp_map))                              # judge-group DIF, factors modelled jointly plot_btl_icc(bt, "E04", group = panel_map)   # curves by judge group  # ------------------------------------------------------- repeated measures -- stack_data(t1, t2)           # change-in-persons: DIF over time rack_data(t1, t2)            # change-in-items

Fits adjacent-categories ordinal extension BTL (Tutz 1986; Agresti 1992): partial-credit structure difference locations thresholds constrained symmetric, tau_k = -tau_(m+1-k), model invariant presentation order. Two categories reproduce BTL exactly; three give Davidson (1970) ties model. margin Optional name column holding extent win ("little", "much", ...), ordered factor increasing values; combined winner assembles graded response without orientation bookkeeping ("B much" means thing whichever column B sits ). Winner values matching neither object ties form middle category. judge Optional name judge column; enables judge fit table clusters sandwich standard errors judge. count Optional name column replication counts (row standing several identical comparisons). order Optional name column giving judge's judgment sequence (timestamps ranks; requires judge). Adds within-judge dependence analysis: exposure effect (advantage, logits, object judge seen one ) carry-effect (pull judge's earlier verdicts object – response dependence sense Marais Andrich 2008), estimated jointly locations reported dependence. title: Fit the Bradley-Terry-Luce model to paired comparisons — btl
code: set.seed(1) beta <- c(A = -1, B = -0.3, C = 0.4, D = 0.9) pairs <- t(combn(names(beta), 2)) d <- data.frame(a = rep(pairs[, 1], each = 30),                 b = rep(pairs[, 2], each = 30)) p <- plogis(beta[d$a] - beta[d$b]) d$win <- ifelse(runif(nrow(d)) < p, d$a, d$b) btl(d, object_a = "a", object_b = "b", winner = "win") #> Bradley-Terry-Luce analysis: 4 objects, 180 comparisons #> Conditional ML: converged in 6 iterations; sandwich SEs #> Object separation index 0.963; pairwise chi-square 1.07 on 3 df, p = 0.783 #>  object location    se comparisons wins fit_resid #>       A   -1.238 0.214          90   16    -0.111 #>       B   -0.354 0.186          90   36     0.526 #>       C    0.448 0.180          90   56    -0.056 #>       D    1.144 0.209          90   72     0.037

code: set.seed(1) beta <- c(A = -1, B = -0.3, C = 0.4, D = 0.9) pr <- t(combn(names(beta), 2)) d <- data.frame(a = rep(pr[, 1], each = 60), b = rep(pr[, 2], each = 60),                 judge = sample(sprintf("J%02d", 1:12), 360, TRUE)) shift <- ifelse(d$judge %in% sprintf("J%02d", 1:6) & d$a == "C", 0.9,          ifelse(d$judge %in% sprintf("J%02d", 1:6) & d$b == "C", -0.9, 0)) p <- plogis(beta[d$a] - beta[d$b] + shift) d$win <- ifelse(runif(nrow(d)) < p, d$a, d$b) f <- btl(d, "a", "b", winner = "win", judge = "judge") grp <- setNames(rep(c("g1", "g2"), each = 6), sprintf("J%02d", 1:12)) btl_dif(f, grp, objects = "C") #> DIF for paired comparisons: 1 factor(s) [group], main effects #> Residual ANOVA per object and term (uniform = term; non-uniform = term x opponent band) #>  object  term F_uniform p_uniform_adj uniform_DIF F_nonuniform p_nonuniform_adj #>       C group    14.495       < 0.001           *        0.081            0.922 #>  nonuniform_DIF #>                 #>  #> Resolved locations (logits; BH over 1 comparison(s); practical 0.50) #>  object  term level_a level_b difference    se     z   p_adj significant #>       C group      g1      g2      1.404 0.271 5.186 < 0.001           * #>  practical #>          *

code: set.seed(1) simP <- function(th, tau) { x <- 0:length(tau); p <- exp(x * th - c(0, cumsum(tau))); p / sum(p) } th <- rnorm(400) X <- sapply(seq(-1, 1, length.out = 6), function(b)   sapply(th, function(t) sample(0:3, 1, prob = simP(t, b + c(-0.8, 0, 0.8))))) colnames(X) <- paste0("R", 1:6) compare_fits(PCM = rasch(X, model = "PCM"), RSM = rasch(X, model = "RSM")) #> Model comparison (reference: PCM) #>  #>  label model persons items two_delta_ll chisq_per_df item_fit_sd person_fit_sd #>    PCM   PCM     400     6                     0.829       0.556         0.835 #>    RSM   RSM     400     6      -22.886        0.884       0.600         0.837 #>    PSI alpha #>  0.745 0.784 #>  0.743 0.784 #> (further columns on the object: loglik, parameters, same_data) #>  #> two_delta_ll is a composite (pairwise) likelihood difference against the reference fit, reported only for fits of the same response data; it is descriptive, not chi-square calibrated. Across different data preparations compare chisq_per_df, the fit residual SDs (ideal 1), PSI, and alpha.

what: Estimate the magnitude of response dependence between two items — dependence_magnitude
title: Estimate the magnitude of response dependence between two items — dependence_magnitude
text: Quantifies strongly dependent item's response follows independent item's response, logits, resolution method Andrich Kreiner (2010; polytomous generalisation Andrich, Humphry Marais 2012), resolution dependent item. dependent item resolved one item per category independent item (carrying responses persons gave category), original items removed, model refitted. dependence magnitude \(d\), threshold \(k\) resolved item category \(x_i\) shifted \(-d\) \(k \le x_i\) \(+d\) otherwise, threshold yields \(\hat d_k = (\hat\delta_{ji(k)}(x_i = k-1) - \hat\delta_{ji(k)}(x_i = k))/2\) \(\hat d\) mean (eq. 24.7 Andrich Marais 2019). resolved items answered disjoint persons, estimates independent, standard error pools threshold variances: \(\hat\sigma^2_k = (\hat\sigma^2_{(k)(k-1)} + \hat\sigma^2_{(k)(k)})/4\), \(V[\hat d] = \bar{\hat\sigma^2_k}/m\) (eqs. 24.9-24.11).

what: Magnitude of multidimensionality from a subtest analysis — dimensionality_magnitude
title: Magnitude of multidimensionality from a subtest analysis — dimensionality_magnitude
text: Estimates strongly two hypothesised subscales measure distinct traits, Andrich's (2016) comparison two reliability calculations: one treating items independent (inflates reliability multidimensionality) one subtest analysis subscale combined single polytomous super-item (absorbs unique subscale variance). bifactor formalisation \(\beta_{ns} = \beta_n + c\,\beta'_{ns}\) (Marais Andrich 2008), \(S\) subscales \(K\) items, $$c^2 = S\,(r_1/r_2 - 1) \frac{SK - 1}{S(K - 1)},$$ latent correlation subscales \(\rho = 1/(1 + c^2)\), \(= S/(S + c^2)\) proportion common (non-unique, non-error) variance. person separation index coefficient alpha versions reported (Andrich Marais 2019, ch. 24).

title: Fit and diagnose a Rasch model by pairwise conditional estimation — rasch
text: data Persons--items integer score matrix (categories 0), data frame also containing ID person-factor columns. Missing values allowed. model Either "PCM" (partial credit) "RSM" (rating scale). id Optional name ID column data, vector IDs; carried person estimates. factors Optional character vector person-factor column names data (DIF analysis), data frame factors. items Optional character vector naming item columns; default every column named id factors. n_groups Number class intervals item-trait chi-square ANOVA item fit. default NULL applies rule Andrich Marais (2019, ch. 15): many intervals least 50 non-extreme persons sample allows, 10, least 2. resolved value stored fit$n_groups. adjust_N Optional reference sample size; supplied, item-trait chi-squares rescaled size (sample-size adjustment sensitivity chi-square large samples). anchors Optional anchor table equating: data frame columns item, k, tau fixing nominated thresholds known values; see pcml. anchors place scale origin comes anchors, person measures directly comparable across separately analysed datasets. na_codes Values read missing. Defaults -1, conventional missing-response code; negative score also treated missing, since valid category scores start zero. key Optional multiple-choice scoring key, three forms. (1) named vector data frame columns item key naming item's correct option: scored 0/1 (case-insensitive trimming; blanks become missing). (2) Double keying: several correct options separated "/" (example "/C"), scoring 1. (3) Polytomous option scoring (Andrich Styles 2011): data frame columns item, option, score assigning integer score every credited option (unlisted options score 0), informative distractors receive partial credit item fitted polytomous; see distractor_rescore evidence-based proposal. Raw responses retained fit$mc distractor_analysis plot_distractors. pc_components NULL (default) estimates every PCM threshold freely. integer 1 4 instead estimates item's thresholds Andrich principal-components reparameterisation (see pcml_pc): 1 = location , 2 = + spread (dispersion model Andrich 1982), 3 = + skewness, 4 = + kurtosis (full principal-components model; Pedler 1987). Useful categories sparsely populated; component estimates returned fit$est$components. PCM , combinable anchors. maxit, tol Newton-Raphson iteration cap convergence tolerance pairwise conditional estimation.

title: Fit and diagnose a Rasch model by pairwise conditional estimation — rasch
text: object class "rasch": list item summary   (items), thresholds (standard errors), person   table (person, including ID factors), score table,   residuals, reliability (psi, psi_noext, item   separation index isi, alpha), targeting, item-trait   statistics (item_trait, item_anova),   summary distribution block (summary_stats: location fit   residual mean/SD/skewness/kurtosis, fit-location correlations,   cell degrees--freedom factor), threshold diagnostics, estimation   details (est).

title: Fit and diagnose a Rasch model by pairwise conditional estimation — rasch
text: fit residual follows Andrich Marais (2019, ch. 23) exactly: standardised residuals squared summed item's (person's) observed cells among non-extreme persons, compared summed cell degrees freedom (model-testing degrees freedom, cells minus estimated parameters, apportioned equally cells), symmetrised log--mean-square transform \(f (\ln Y^2 - \ln f)/\sqrt{V[Y^2]}\) model-based variance \(V[Y^2] = \sum (C_4/V^2 - 1)\). Values approximately N(0,1) fit; conventional flagging value 2.5 (Andrich Marais 2019, ch. 15). Negative values indicate -discrimination (Guttman-like responses), positive values -discrimination.

text: judge_surprise() plot_btl_judge_map(): paired-comparison counterpart kidmap. judge ability condition , reference consensus object scale; object judge met gets standardised residual saying much less judge favoured location predicts. strong object judge -rated, weak object -rated, unexpected judgement. catches systematic bias – distinct failure mode noise judge fit judge consistency flag. Shiny app Persons > Judge fit panel now master-detail: judges table drives selected judge’s map, mirroring person -> kidmap.

title: rasch 1.4.0
text: btl_transitivity() counts circular triads (preference loops: beats B, B beats C, C beats ) chance rate, reports Kendall’s coefficient consistency complete designs, gives judge’s consistency (Kendall & Babington Smith 1940) – single-dimension check, judge-fit analogue. btl_dimensionality() decomposes skew-symmetric object--object residual preference matrix Gower (1977) bimensions – paired-comparison counterpart residual PCA – judges leading bimension model-simulated noise reference, coherent residual “swirl” (second attribute steering contests) distinguished noise. plot_btl_scree(), plot_btl_dim_map(), plot_btl_transitivity() display ; Shiny app’s Independence > Trait tab now carries BTL fit (previously hid paired comparisons).

title: rasch 1.3.1
text: package now called rasch (developed working title rmt). name rmt belongs unrelated package CRAN, rasch says plainly – mirrors mirt::mirt() rasch::rasch(). functions, arguments, results change; package library name. auxiliary result classes unified rasch_ prefix (rasch_btl, rasch_dif, …) match fit classes.

title: rasch 1.3.0
text: full statistical audit independence, invariance, paired- comparison procedures, fixes throughout. Paired comparisons: btl() now interrogates within-judge dependence – dependence_data holds every comparison’s exposure carry-covariates (count-weighted), plot_btl_dependence() displays effect partial-residual curve, separated effect (one-sided informative comparisons) set aside note instead reported runaway estimate. graded engine’s threshold--dependence Hessian block corrected (free-threshold fits five categories order column now converge), rows missing judge dropped note, category checks re-run boundary objects removed, dichotomous path now routes one (vectorised) estimator. btl_dif() holds fitted dependence effects fixed residual moments resolution refits (dependence longer absorbed spurious judge-group DIF), weights count-aggregated comparisons exactly expanded rows, restricts supersession group terms, notes every significant term resolve. dif_anova() keeps ANOVA term error stratum, mixed (within-subject) design missing responses longer un-flags real DIF; factors named like internals (ci, f1) safe; term’s class-interval crossing longer supersedes (resolve_dif() splits items uniform non-uniform DIF); mixed designs say plainly Tukey comparisons unavailable; dif_size() notes within-person factor makes standard errors conservative. plot_scree()’s parallel-analysis reference now model-simulated (responses drawn calibrated model, persons re-estimated), model-true data sits reference instead always “showing structure” (Raiche 2005; Chou & Wang 2010). Shiny app: judge-group DIF results freeze run’s factors alpha (later sidebar edits silently regroup overlay break reproducible snippets), superseded terms shown, within-judge dependence panel explains dropped effects, fit flags consistent every table (fit residual |2.5|; outfit 0.7-1.3; infit 0.8-1.2).

title: rasch 1.2.0
text: plot_pca_biplot() draws item loadings first two residual principal components – pair usually carries interpretable contrast – equal axes, coloured sign PC1 loading. residual_correlations() now also returns adjusted-Q3 star_matrix (Q3 less average -diagonal), plot_resid_cor() gains stat argument colour either raw Q3 adjusted Q3*, drawing lower triangle . Shiny app: trait- local-dependence pages pair table plot (loadings PC1-PC2 biplot; Q3 Q3* matrices heatmaps), raw adjusted correlations flagged rule (|Q3| Q3*), every data-restructuring action (subtest, item split, automatic DIF resolution) offers -place reset original data. dif_anova() now single DIF analysis--variance function. One factor analysed one-way; several factors modelled jointly – statistically correct treatment – main effects default factor--factor interactions optional (effects = "factorial"). handles within-subject factors (repeated-measures / mixed ANOVA) returns classed object summary, full terms table, Tukey comparisons. separate dif_anova_factorial() removed. resolve_dif() resolves DIF iteratively item splitting, largest effect first, clear artificial DIF.

title: rasch 1.0.0
text: First stable release. package delivers complete Rasch Measurement Theory workflow built entirely published measurement theory, pairwise conditional estimation core Shiny interface exposes every analysis reproducing R code attached every output.

title: rasch 1.0.0
text: rasch(): dichotomous polytomous (partial credit rating scale) Rasch models pairwise conditional maximum likelihood (Andrich & Luo 2003; Zwinderman 1995), Godambe sandwich standard errors, sum-zero identification, Warm (1989) weighted likelihood person estimation, principal-component threshold parameterisation estimation option. rasch_mfrm(): many-facet models additive facet severities item--facet interactions, accepting wide (one column per item) long data. rasch_efrm(): extended frame--reference model (Humphry) frame units estimated within-frame pairwise conditional likelihood. btl(): paired comparisons conditional form dichotomous Rasch model (Bradley & Terry 1952; Andrich 1978), including adjacent-categories graded extension (Tutz 1986; Agresti 1992) symmetric thresholds – Davidson (1970) ties model three-category case – winner-plus-margin data entry, judge-clustered errors, within-judge exposure carry-effects estimated jointly locations.

title: rasch 1.0.0
text: test--fit suite follows Andrich & Marais (2019): log--mean-square fit residuals apportioned degrees freedom, item-trait chi-square automatically sized, tie-preserving class intervals per-interval detail, ANOVA item fit, person fit. Unidimensionality: residual principal components parallel analysis, Smith (2002) subset t-test, magnitude estimation. Local dependence: Yen’s (1984) Q3 Christensen, Makransky & Horton (2017) flagging convention, response-dependence magnitude (Andrich & Kreiner), subtest formation, spread test. Guessing: tailored analysis origin-equated comparison calibrations. DIF: two-way residual ANOVA per factor, joint factorial analysis compact uniform/non-uniform summary, resolved DIF magnitudes logits familywise control practical-significance criterion, planned contrasts derived factor structure (repeated-measures support via person-level residual scores), class intervals sized cells analysis actually uses. paired comparisons, btl_dif() tests object--judge-group interaction two routes. Equating invariance: common-item comparison drift flags, calibration anchoring.

title: rasch 1.0.0
text: complete base-graphics plot suite: expected value curves observed class-interval means, category threshold probability curves, person-item threshold distribution, Wright maps, kidmaps (Wright, Mead & Ludlow 1980), object characteristic curves paired comparisons, threshold item maps, test characteristic information curves, residual displays, Guttman scalograms – adjustable class intervals scale ranges, batch export multi-page PDF ZIP publication resolution. fit_summary_table() targeting_table() return headline statistics tidy tables; save_outputs() writes every table plot disk; report_html() produces single-file HTML report. run_app() launches Shiny interface: model-adaptive navigation, reproducing R code beneath every output, one-click export.
